# The Merton model



The Merton model is useful when we are interested in evaluating the credit risk of public firms. Let $T$ denote the debt maturity date, $D$ the promised debt payment due at that date, and $V_T$ the value of the firm's assets at maturity. In Merton, default occurs at $T$ if the firm does not have enough asset value to cover the promised debt payment:

$$
V_T<D.
$$

Here, $D$ is the promised debt payment due at maturity. The current market value of debt is a different object. The distinction is essential because $D$ is the default boundary: it is the amount the firm must be able to pay at $T$. In Merton, the market value of debt today can be inferred after valuing the firm's claims, for example as the difference between the asset value $V_0$ and the equity value $E_0$.

The model estimates how likely that event is, under the model assumptions, and it also produces other credit-risk quantities. The relevant object is a probability assigned to a future event. In this setting, a single firm defaults once or survives over the horizon, so Merton treats default as a future event and asks how much probability the model assigns to $V_T<D$. The model can be used as a tool to evaluate changes in the firm's balance sheet and to understand how those changes affect credit risk.

The philosophy of these models goes back to @black1973pricing, @merton1973theory and @merton1974pricing. They treat the firm's financial claims, especially equity and debt, as contingent claims written on the assets of the firm. A contingent claim is a financial claim whose payoff depends on what happens to an underlying variable. In Merton, the underlying variable is the future value of the firm's assets, $V_T$. The assets are the underlying source of value; equity and debt are the claims whose payoffs depend on whether $V_T$ is above or below the promised debt payment $D$.

The same structural-credit language remains active. Recent research extends structural models by allowing the firm's debt policy, and therefore the default boundary, to move over time; moving boundaries change predicted credit spreads [@FeldhutterSchaefer2023DebtDynamics]. In industry practice, Moody's 2025 EDF-X example shows the related use of forward-looking PD signals to identify credit deterioration before a bankruptcy event [@MoodysFirstBrands2025]. This chapter keeps the classic Merton model because it makes the core objects visible: asset value, promised debt payment, asset volatility, distance to default, and the conversion from those objects to a model-implied PD.

The route through the chapter is deliberately sequential. First, we use a two-state example to explain why risk-neutral probabilities are pricing weights distinct from real-world default frequencies. Second, we replicate Hull's Merton equations and solve for the unobserved asset value $V_0$ and asset volatility $\sigma_V$. Third, we connect the fitted model to the risk-neutral default probability $N(-d_2)$. Fourth, we simulate risk-neutral asset paths to make the terminal-default event visible. Fifth, we read equity as a call option on the firm's assets. Finally, we use sensitivity and capital-structure scenarios to ask how changes in equity value, volatility, promised debt payment, maturity, and the risk-free rate affect the model-implied probability of default.

## Why risk-neutral valuation works here

The Merton model links today, $t=0$, with debt maturity, $t=T$. At $t=0$, market values such as equity value are observed. At $t=T$, the firm either repays the promised debt payment $D$ or defaults. The key future variable is the value of the firm's assets at maturity, $V_T$.

At maturity, equity is the residual claim on the firm's assets.

$$
E_T=\max(V_T-D,0).
$$

This payoff rule is the credit-risk logic of the model. If $V_T>D$, the firm can repay the debt and equity holders receive $V_T-D$. If $V_T<D$, the firm cannot repay the debt in full and equity is worth zero. Therefore, survival corresponds to $E_T>0$, and default corresponds to $E_T=0$.

At $t=0$, $V_T$ and $E_T$ are future random variables. The information available today is current market information. In the full Merton implementation, the observed equity value $E_0$ and equity volatility $\sigma_E$ help us infer the unobserved asset value $V_0$ and asset volatility $\sigma_V$. Those inferred asset quantities then determine the model-implied probability of the future default event $V_T<D$. That full estimation workflow is what the rest of the chapter develops.

::: {.merton-subhead}
A two-state pricing example
:::

The one-period example in this section is simpler. It takes $V_0=100$ as the known value of the firm's assets today. The example represents uncertainty about the future asset value with only two possible maturity values, $V_u=110$ and $V_d=85$, leaving asset volatility $\sigma_V$ aside. The spread between these two values is the toy version of asset risk. This lets us focus only on the valuation logic.

Because this is only a teaching device, we choose the two maturity asset values by hand. The full Merton model will replace these two outcomes with a continuous distribution for $V_T$.

There are two ways to value the future equity payoff $E_T$ today. A real-world valuation would use real-world probabilities and a risky discount rate. That route can be useful for a forecasting question. For pricing, however, each payoff needs a discount rate that matches its own risk. The example below will show why that discount rate becomes payoff-specific.

Risk-neutral valuation uses a pricing method based on no-arbitrage. It replaces the real-world probabilities with pricing probabilities, denoted here by $q$ and $1-q$, chosen so that the current value of the firm's assets, $V_0$, is reproduced when expected future asset values are discounted at the risk-free rate. In this one-period example, that current asset value is $V_0=100$. The real world remains risky; the adjustment for risk is moved into the probabilities used for pricing.

To see the mechanics, suppose the firm has debt $D=100$. The table shows the two possible asset values at maturity and the corresponding equity value.

<table class="caption-top table merton-state-table">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 9%">
<col style="width: 21%">
<col style="width: 34%">
<col style="width: 36%">
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: center;">State</th>
<th style="text-align: center;"><span class="math inline">\\(V_T\\)</span></th>
<th style="text-align: center;"><span class="math inline">\\(E_T\\)</span></th>
<th style="text-align: center;">Credit state</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center;">Good</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="math inline">\\(V_u=110\\)</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="math inline">\\(E_u=\\max(110-100,0)=10\\)</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">Survival if <span class="math inline">\\(V_u>D\\)</span></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center;">Bad</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="math inline">\\(V_d=85\\)</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="math inline">\\(E_d=\\max(85-100,0)=0\\)</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">Default if <span class="math inline">\\(V_d<D\\)</span></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

At this point, the asset values in the example are known: $V_0=100$, $V_u=110$, and $V_d=85$. Now set the one-period risk-free rate to $R=5\%$. The only unknown in the pricing equation is $q$, the risk-neutral probability of the good state.

We choose $q$ so that the known asset value today, $V_0=100$, is equal to the risk-free discounted expected asset value at maturity:

$$
V_0=\frac{qV_u+(1-q)V_d}{1+R}.
$$

Now substitute the known values $V_0=100$, $V_u=110$, $V_d=85$, and $R=5\%$:

$$
100=\frac{q(110)+(1-q)(85)}{1.05}.
$$

Now $q$ is the only unknown. Multiply both sides by 1.05:

$$
100(1.05)=q(110)+(1-q)(85).
$$

Then solve for $q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
105 &= 110q+85-85q \\
105 &= 85+25q \\
20 &= 25q \\
q &= 0.80.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
q=0.80,
\qquad
1-q=0.20.
$$

Using these pricing probabilities, the expected asset value at maturity is

$$
E^Q[V_T]=0.80(110)+0.20(85)=105.
$$

This also equals $100(1.05)$ by construction. Under the risk-neutral probability, the expected asset return is the risk-free rate, while the realized future asset value remains uncertain.

The future asset value itself is still state-dependent. It is 110 in the good state and 85 in the bad state. The number 105 is not a third possible value for $V_T$; it is only the expected asset value under the pricing probability. That is where the risk remains. If the asset payoff were risk-free, $V_T$ would be known in advance. Here, $V_T$ is uncertain, even though its risk-neutral expected value is determined by $V_0(1+R)$.

The probability $q=0.80$ is a pricing probability. It is determined by the current asset value, the two possible future asset values, and the risk-free rate inside this example. A physical or real-world probability would ask about the actual chance of the good state.

Once $q$ prices the assets, the same $q$ can price equity because equity is a payoff written on those assets.

$$
E_0=\frac{qE_u+(1-q)E_d}{1+R}.
$$

Substituting the numbers gives

$$
E_0=\frac{0.80(10)+0.20(0)}{1.05}=7.6190.
$$

This is the no-arbitrage value today of equity in the one-period example: $E_0=7.6190$. The value can be verified directly by constructing a portfolio that reproduces the equity payoff. Let $\Delta$ be the number of units of the firm's assets held today, and let $C$ be the cash position invested in the risk-free asset. If $C<0$, the portfolio borrows at the risk-free rate.

To match the equity payoff in both states, the portfolio must satisfy

$$
\begin{aligned}
110\Delta+1.05C &= 10, \\
85\Delta+1.05C &= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first gives

$$
25\Delta=10
\Rightarrow
\Delta=0.40.
$$

Using the bad-state equation,

$$
85(0.40)+1.05C=0
\Rightarrow
C=-32.3810.
$$

The negative cash position means that the portfolio borrows 32.3810 today. The cost of this replicating portfolio today is therefore

$$
0.40(100)+(-32.3810)=7.6190.
$$

At maturity, the portfolio pays $0.40(110)-32.3810(1.05)=10$ in the good state and $0.40(85)-32.3810(1.05)=0$ in the bad state. Since it reproduces the equity payoff exactly, its current cost must equal the no-arbitrage equity value.

::: {.merton-subhead}
Why real-world probabilities are not enough for pricing
:::

Could we use a real-world probability and a risky discount rate? Yes, if the objective were to estimate a real-world expected return or a real-world default frequency. That is a valid empirical question. Merton is answering a pricing question here.

For example, suppose for illustration that the real-world probability of the good state is $p=0.90$. The real-world expected asset value would be

$$
0.90(110)+0.10(85)=107.50.
$$

If the correct expected return on the assets were 7.5%, this would also recover the current asset value.

$$
100=\frac{107.50}{1.075}.
$$

This works for the asset itself because the 7.5% return was chosen for the asset payoff. But equity is a different payoff. With $p=0.90$, the real-world expected equity payoff would be

$$
E^P[E_T]=0.90(10)+0.10(0)=9.
$$

There are two tempting mistakes here. The first mistake would be to treat this expected future payoff as the value of equity today. That would set $E_0=9$. But 9 is not an arbitrage-free price. The replicating portfolio above costs 7.6190 and produces exactly the same future payoff as equity. If equity traded for 9, one could sell equity for 9 and buy the replicating portfolio for 7.6190. The cash flow today would be

$$
9-7.6190=1.3810.
$$

At maturity, the replicating portfolio pays exactly what is needed to cover the short equity position: 10 in the good state and 0 in the bad state. The future cash flow is zero in both states, so the 1.3810 received today is an arbitrage profit.

The second mistake would be to discount the equity payoff at 7.5%, because 7.5% was the expected return on the asset, not on equity. Equity is more levered and riskier than the asset. If we used 7.5% anyway, we would get

$$
\frac{9}{1.075}=8.3721,
$$

which still differs from the no-arbitrage equity value obtained above, $E_0=7.6190$. To value equity with real-world probabilities, we would need the correct risky discount rate for the equity payoff itself. In this toy example, once we already know the no-arbitrage equity value $E_0=7.6190$, we can infer that rate:

$$
r_E=\frac{E^P[E_T]}{E_0}-1
=\frac{9}{7.6190}-1
=18.125\%.
$$

But this is an implied rate obtained after knowing the no-arbitrage price, $E_0=7.6190$. It is not an independent input we had before valuing equity. If we wanted to use real-world probabilities as the starting point, we would need to estimate this equity discount rate first.

This is the practical advantage of the risk-neutral route for pricing. The value $q=0.80$ is a pricing probability consistent with today's asset value and the risk-free rate. In this two-state complete-market example, once those probabilities price the asset, they can also price the equity payoff written on that asset by discounting at the risk-free rate. More generally, the Merton model uses the same no-arbitrage logic under its model assumptions. For real-world default forecasting, a real-world probability may be the better object. For market-implied valuation and the Merton default probability used in this chapter, the risk-neutral probability is the relevant object.

This distinction gives us two different probabilities.

| Question | Probability | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Which probability weights default states when valuing equity today? | $P^Q(V_T<D)$ | Risk-neutral or pricing probability |
| Which probability describes the firm's real-world default event by $T$? | $P^P(V_T<D)$ | Real-world, physical, or historical probability |

The Merton model in this chapter is designed for the first question. In practice, $E_0$ can be read as the firm's market capitalization. Together with observed equity volatility $\sigma_E$, the promised debt payment $D$, maturity $T$, the risk-free rate, and the model assumptions, $E_0$ helps us solve for $V_0$ and $\sigma_V$. From those asset quantities, the model gives the risk-neutral probability $P^Q(V_T<D)$. Its practical advantage is that it is tied to current market prices through the model, avoiding the separate estimation of a real-world default frequency and a risky discount rate for equity.

The real-world probability $P^P(V_T<D)$ asks a different empirical question. One way to estimate it would be to use historical data on many firms over many years, match the current firm to firms with similar risk characteristics, and estimate how often comparable firms defaulted over similar horizons. The matching variables could include leverage, asset or equity volatility, firm size, rating, sector, maturity structure, and macroeconomic conditions. Another route would be to estimate the real-world asset return process directly. Both approaches introduce estimation error, while the risk-neutral probability is implied by observed market prices once the model assumptions are accepted.

In the one-period example, the risk-neutral probability of default is the pricing probability of the bad state.

$$
P^Q(V_T<D)=1-q=0.20.
$$

Merton uses a continuous risk-neutral distribution for $V_T$ with many possible future asset values, but the logic is the same. Equity is valued as a call option on the firm's assets, default happens when $V_T<D$, and the model gives a risk-neutral, market-implied default probability. In this chapter, unless explicitly stated otherwise, `pd` means the Merton risk-neutral/implied probability of default.

After estimating the Merton model, we will return to this idea with the actual numbers from Hull's example. At that point, we will introduce the notation $N(-d_2)$. For now, the important idea is simpler. In the generic one-period example, the risk-neutral default probability is the pricing weight of the bad branch, $1-q=0.20$.

The following sections develop the structural credit-risk workflow from risk-neutral valuation to Merton-model estimation, default probabilities, and capital-structure scenarios.

{{< include 02-merton-estimation.qmd >}}

{{< include 02-merton-inside-view.qmd >}}

{{< include 02-merton-pd-parameters.qmd >}}

{{< include 02-merton-capital-structure.qmd >}}
